:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Each felony in Oklahoma has a statutory punishment; there are currently no “classes” or “levels” of crimes as found in other states. Inmates convicted of crimes committed on or after July 1, 1998 are eligible for parole release after serving one-third of their sentence, unless they are serving a life sentence without the possibility of parole. Inmates serving consecutive sentences are not eligible for parole consideration until one-third of the consecutive sentence has been served. However, Oklahoma has enacted truth in sentencing laws which require inmates convicted of certain crimes to serve at least 85% of their sentence of imprisonment without any reduction due to sentencing credits. Some crimes, such as first-degree rape, have statutory minimum penalties. There are also statutory minimum penalties for second or subsequent convictions for certain crimes.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 6,241 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 29% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Unknown people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 60% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 93% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 35-44 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 29% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date.
